topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the lecture hall seemed particularly harsh today, casting a sickly yellow pallor over the worn textbooks scattered across my desk. I traced the faded ink of a syllabus, the lines blurring under the pressure of my thumb. Every time I glanced toward the administrative bulletin board, a familiar knot tightened in my chest, a dull, persistent ache that refused to dissipate. The whispers in the hallway had grown louder lately—murmurs about budget cuts, about the future of the institution. I kept my gaze fixed on my laptop screen, the glow reflecting in my eyes, trying to focus on the complex coding assignment, but the solution remained elusive. When Professor Davies finally announced the meeting, his voice was flat, devoid of its usual warmth. The air in the room seemed to thicken, pressing down on my shoulders. I didn't look up when the dean started speaking, just fiddled with the edge of my notebook, the sound of the pen scratching against the paper sounding unnervingly loud in the sudden silence.

[story 2]

I walked the familiar campus pathways with a heavy tread, the gravel crunching under my worn sneakers sounding like footsteps echoing my own internal turmoil. The autumn leaves, usually a vibrant spectacle, felt brittle and dying beneath my feet. Each familiar landmark—the ivy-covered library, the sprawling quad—now seemed overlaid with a strange sense of finality. My shoulders were perpetually hunched, a defensive posture against an invisible chill that seemed to seep into my bones. I stopped by the old clock tower, watching the hands sweep across the face, marking time that felt increasingly irrelevant. A sudden wave of nausea washed over me; I gripped the strap of my backpack so tightly my knuckles turned white. "It's just a phase," I muttered to the empty air, the words tasting like ash. But the official notices plastered on the notice boards, the stark lettering announcing the closure, kept reasserting a cold, hard reality.

[story 3]

The coffee tasted bitter, a sharp, unwelcome jolt that did little to settle the restlessness churning beneath my ribs. I stared out the window of the student union, watching the bustling activity of the campus fade into a muted backdrop. I picked at the frayed edge of my sweater, a repetitive, anxious motion. Every casual conversation I overheard—laughter, plans for the weekend—felt like a performance I wasn't invited to. My mind kept cycling through scenarios: the logistics of moving, the sudden void where routine used to be, the sheer inconvenience of starting over. A tightness constricted my throat, making it difficult to draw a full breath. When a friend approached, offering a bright, forced smile, I managed a tight nod, my expression carefully blank, the effort of maintaining composure draining every ounce of my energy. I just wanted the noise to stop.
